content.text:
<analysis>
a. Event type: Pull request opened (feat/review-psepk-iaam), requesting code review and feedback
b. This is a request for code review feedback - the user wants me to analyze the PR for code quality, bugs, performance, security, and test coverage
c. Key information:
   - PR adds salvaged AIGR review for P. putida Iaam gene 
   - 5 new files added: ai-review.yaml, deep-research-falcon.md, goa.tsv, notes.md, uniprot.txt
   - Copilot reviewer found issues: duplicated content, broken DOI URLs, citation mismatches
   - This is gene annotation review data, not executable code
d. Main tasks:
   - Review the 5 added files for quality and adherence to project standards
   - Check against CLAUDE.md guidelines for gene review format
   - Assess data integrity and completeness
   - Validate YAML structure against schema
e. Plan:
   - Read CLAUDE.md to understand project requirements
   - Examine each file for compliance with standards
   - Run validation if possible (just validate command)
   - Provide comprehensive feedback on issues found
f. May need to install dependencies to run validation tools
</analysis>

input.body: ### PR Review in Progress Reviewing the P. putida Iaam gene annotation review PR. I'll provide feedback on code quality, potential issues, and adherence to project standards. ### Todo List - [ ] Read project guidelines and schema requirements - [ ] Review file structure and naming conventions - [ ] Validate YAML structure and content - [ ] Check data integrity and completeness - [ ] Address issues identified by Copilot reviewer - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback and recommendations --- [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23419538489)

content.content:
1→# AI instructions for ai-gene-review
     2→
     3→## Project Overview
     4→
     5→This project is for managing AI reviews of gene annotations. The overall goal is to both
     6→review existing GO annotations using strict defined criteria, and to summarize the resulting
     7→high quality annotations.
     8→
     9→## Repository layout
    10→
    11→src/
    12→  ai_gene_review/
    13→    schema/
    14→      gene_review.yaml <-- linkml schema for review docs
    15→genes/
    16→  human/
    17→    CFAP300/
    18→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- keep your notes here
    19→      CFAP300-ai-review.yaml <-- the output of your AI review
    20→      CFAP300-notes.md <-- your own notes, edit this, use it like a journal and keep appending.
    21→      CFAP300-uniprot.txt <-- the uniprot record downloaded from the UniProt website (do not edit this file)
    22→      CFAP300-goa.tsv <-- the GO annotation downloaded from the QuickGO API (do not edit this file, no need to view it)
    23→      CFAP300-deep-research.md <-- the deep research for the gene review (do not edit this file, it is automatically generated for you)
    24→      CFAP300-citations.md <-- the citations for the gene review (do not edit this file)
    25→      CFAP300-bioinformatics/ <-- the folder for any bioinformatics analyses (create this if necessary)
    26→      CFAP300-predictions-review.yaml <-- review of computational predictions NOT in GOA (optional)
    27→    <other genes>/
    28→      ...
    29→  worm/
    30→    lrx-1/
    31→  SCHPO/  <-- other orgs use uniprot species codes
    32→      ...
    33→publications/
    34→  PMID_123456.md <-- cached publication text (this should be available for you, DO NOT EDIT)
    35→reactome/
    36→  R-HSA-NNNNNN.md <- cached reactome entry
    37→
    38→You can regenerate the derived files by running commands like:
    39→
    40→```
    41→just fetch-gene human CFAP300 
    42→```
    43→
    44→This makes the uniprot, goa, and publications files. It will also create a stub for the ai-review.yaml if it doesn't exist.
    45→
    46→After making edits to the ai-review.yaml file, you can validate the file by running:
    47→
    48→```
    49→just validate human CFAP300
    50→```
    51→
    52→This ensures that the yaml file is consistent with the schema in `src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml`, and also
    53→that it implements best practices.
    54→
    55→To validate all files, run:
    56→
    57→```
    58→just validate-all
    59→```
    60→
    61→files will be written to reports/validation-all.tsv 
    62→
    63→* All gene symbols follow the standard nomenclature for that species. For human, should be HGNC symbols.
    64→
    65→## Gene Review Guidelines
    66→
    67→Your job is to review existing annotations for a gene, following GO guidelines, and synthesize these with what is known in the literature,
    68→and what can be inferred bioinformatically. The general structure of the review is as follows:
    69→
    70→```yaml
    71→id: <uniprot id>
    72→gene_symbol: <HGNC symbol>
    73→taxon:
    74→  id: <NCBI taxon id>
    75→  label: <taxon label>
    76→description: <your own summary of the gene>
    77→references:
    78→  <all relevant reference, including both literature and bioinformatics sources; includes all but not limited to those used in existing_annotations>
    79→existing_annotations:
    80→  <review of existing annotations, using the GO guidelines; should include all annotations from goa.csv>
    81→core_functions:
    82→  <your own summary of the core functions of the gene>
    83→proposed_new_terms:
    84→  <suggestions for new GO terms>
    85→suggested_questions:
    86→  <suggested questions for experts>
    87→suggested_experiments:
    88→  <suggested experiments to elucidate gene function>
    89→```
    90→
    91→See the schema and existing files for more details.
    92→
    93→When creating a new review, always make sure files are in place:
    94→
    95→`just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`
    96→
    97→This creates the uniprot, goa files, and a stub for the gene review.
    98→
    99→The next thing is for you to do deep research on that gene and make a GENE-notes.md file, with citations inline (e.g [PMID:12345 title, "<supporting text>"]). In some cases this will have been done ahead of time for you.
   100→
   101→## Reviewing existing annotations
   102→
   103→You should start with the ai-review.yaml file, where stubs are created for you (from `just fetch-gene <organism> <gene>`). Any PMID cited should be in the publications folder, e.g. `publications/PMID_123456.md`. Review these.
   104→
   105→A deep research file should already be present in the gene folder, e.g. `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-deep-research-PROVIDER.md` (where PROVIDER is openai, falcon, perplexity, etc.). This should contain a summary of the literature, and any other information you found useful for the review. There may be irrelevant information in the citations, that is OK. If this is not present, you can generate one using:
   106→
   107→```bash
   108→just deep-research SPECIES GENE --provider perplexity
   109→```
   110→
   111→This will create a file like `GENE-deep-research-perplexity.md`. You can also make your own notes in `genes/SPECIES/GENE/GENE-notes.md`.
   112→
   113→Be aware that many GO terms are over-annotations. You should also not take existing annotations as gospel, whether experimental or bioinformatic.
   114→always use a holistic synthesized picture of the gene function that you have obtained from your research.
   115→
   116→```yaml
   117→ActionEnum:
   118→    permissible_values:
   119→      ACCEPT:
   120→        description: Accept the existing annotation as-is, no modifications, and retain as representing the core function of the gene
   121→      KEEP_AS_NON_CORE:
   122→        description: Keep the existing annotation as-is, but mark it as non-core. For pleiotropic genes, this may be the developmental
   123→          processes, or other processes that are not the core function of the gene.
   124→      REMOVE:
   125→        description: Remove the existing annotation, as it is unlikely to be correct based on combined evidence
   126→      MODIFY:
   127→        description: The essence of the annotation is sound, but there are better terms to use (use in combination with proposed_replacement_terms).
   128→          if the term is too general, then MODIFY should be used, with a proposed replacement term for the correct specific function.
   129→          sometimes terms can also be overly specific and contorted, so in some cases you might want to generalize
   130→      MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED:
   131→        description: The term is not entirely wrong, but likely represents an over-annotation of the gene
   132→      UNDECIDED:
   133→        description: The annotation is not clear, and the reviewer is not sure what to do with it. ALWAYS USE THIS IF YOU ARE UNABLE TO ACCESS
   134→          RELEVANT PUBLICATIONS
   135→      NEW:
   136→        
   137→```      
   138→
   139→## Tools
   140→
   141→Use the OLS MCP to find relevant ontology terms, if the terms you need are not in existing_annotations.
   142→
   143→## Curation guidelines
   144→
   145→Avoid the term `protein binding`, this doesn't tell us anything about the actual function. Instead find a more
   146→informative MF term (e.g for adapter function)
   147→
   148→## Isoform and Negation Tracking
   149→
   150→The system tracks isoform-specific GO annotations and NOT (negated) annotations:
   151→
   152→```yaml
   153→existing_annotations:
   154→# Isoform-specific annotation
   155→- term:
   156→    id: GO:0045892
   157→    label: negative regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   158→  evidence_type: IDA
   159→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   160→  isoform: P19544-1  # UniProt isoform ID - only present for isoform-specific annotations
   161→  review:
   162→    summary: ...
   163→
   164→# NOT annotation (negated)
   165→- term:
   166→    id: GO:0045893
   167→    label: positive regulation of DNA-templated transcription
   168→  evidence_type: IDA
   169→  original_reference_id: PMID:9815658
   170→  negated: true  # This is a NOT annotation
   171→  isoform: P19544-1
   172→  review:
   173→    summary: WT1 does NOT activate transcription...
   174→```
   175→
   176→- **`isoform`**: UniProt isoform ID (e.g., `P19544-1`) - indicates what isoform was tested
   177→- **`negated`**: Set to `true` for NOT annotations (e.g., `NOT|involved_in` qualifier in GOA)
   178→
   179→**Important**: Just because an annotation was made on a specific isoform doesn't mean the function is isoform-specific.
   180→The `isoform` field indicates what was tested, not necessarily what is unique to that isoform.
   181→
   182→To backfill isoform info on existing reviews:
   183→```bash
   184→just backfill-isoforms human WT1
   185→just backfill-isoforms-organism human  # all genes in organism
   186→```
   187→
   188→See `docs/isoform_tracking.md` for full documentation and `projects/ISOFORMS.md` for genes with notable isoform-specific functions.
   189→
   190→## Bioinformatics analyses
   191→
   192→In some cases, it may be useful to do additional bioinformatics analyses. To validate gene function. Here are some guidelines:
   193→
   194→For a gene-specific analysis, the folder is `<genes/specues/GENE>-bioinformatics/`. You should make this be reproducible. You can do a `uv init`
   195→here and `uv add` for any packages you need.
   196→
   197→Never hardcode the results of the script, it's OK for a script to return inconclusive results. Never ever make up results.
   198→
   199→**IMPORTANT**: DO NOT write scripts that just tell users to go to a website. If you cannot programmatically call a tool (e.g., requires API keys, browser interaction, or authentication you don't have), then:
   200→1. Be honest about this limitation upfront
   201→2. Write scripts that can parse/process the output from the tool if the user runs it manually
   202→3. Never write misleading code that pretends to do something it doesn't actually do
   203→
   204→You can summarize the results of the bioinformatics analyses in the `<GENE>-bioinformatics/RESULTS.md` file. This can be referenced as any other reference
   205→as e.g `file:<SPECIES>/<GENE>/bioinformatics/RESULTS.md`.
   206→
   207→## Reviewing Computational Predictions
   208→
   209→The `GENE-predictions-review.yaml` file reviews computational/ML predictions that are NOT
   210→in the curated GOA/UniProt annotations. This is separate from `existing_annotations` in the
   211→main review file.
   212→
   213→Use the `PredictionReview` class (validated with `-C PredictionReview`):
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→uv run linkml-validate -s src/ai_gene_review/schema/gene_review.yaml -C PredictionReview genes/ECOLI/yciO/yciO-predictions-review.yaml
   217→```
   218→
   219→### Prediction sources
   220→
   221→Track predictions from: DeepECTF, PANTHER/IBA, InterPro2GO, CLEAN, GloEC, MAPred, or any
   222→other computational method that produces GO or EC predictions.
   223→
   224→### Assessment categories (from de Crécy-Lagard et al. 2025, PMID:40703034)
   225→
   226→| Code | CS | Description |
   227→|------|-----|-------------|
   228→| COR  | 2   | Correct novel prediction |
   229→| CNN  | 2   | Correct but Not Novel (already in training data) |
   230→| LSP  | 2   | Less Precise than existing annotation |
   231→| UNC  | 1   | Uncertain - cannot validate or refute |
   232→| PLI  | 0   | Paralog Incorrect - wrong paralog subfamily |
   233→| NPI  | 0   | Nonparalog Incorrect - refuted by evidence |
   234→| REP  | 0   | Repetition - frequency-biased prediction |
   235→
   236→### Error types
   237→
   238→- `PARALOG_OVERANNOTATION` - Wrong paralog subfamily (Type 6)
   239→- `PATHWAY_CONTEXT_IGNORED` - Predicted pathway absent from organism
   240→- `FREQUENCY_BIAS` - Model defaults to high-frequency training labels
   241→- `IN_VITRO_NOT_IN_VIVO` - In vitro activity doesn't match biological function
   242→- `TRAINING_DATA_CONTAMINATION` - Prediction already in training data
   243→- See schema for full list
   244→
   245→## General guidelines
   246→
   247→* NEVER guess identifiers for terms, genes, publications. Always use the relevant tools or MCPS, or look them up in derived files.
   248→* For files `<GENE>-notes.md`, use literature deep search, and always record provenance for assertions, e.g `[PMID:12345 "<supporting text>"]`
   249→
   250→## Support code
   251→
   252→There is also support code in `src/ai_gene_review`, see the CLAUDE.md file in that directory for more details
   253→on best practices for working with the code.
   254→
   255→
   256→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.body: ### PR Review in Progress Reviewing the P. putida Iaam gene annotation review PR. I'll provide feedback on code quality, potential issues, and adherence to project standards. ### Todo List - [x] Read project guidelines and schema requirements - [ ] Review file structure and naming conventions - [ ] Validate YAML structure and content - [ ] Check data integrity and completeness - [ ] Address issues identified by Copilot reviewer - [ ] Provide comprehensive feedback and recommendations --- [View job run](https://github.com/ai4curation/ai-gene-review/actions/runs/23419538489)

content.text: Now let me examine the files in the PR to assess compliance with the project guidelines:

content.content:
1→id: Q88D45
     2→gene_symbol: PP_4983
     3→aliases:
     4→- iaaM
     5→product_type: PROTEIN
     6→status: DRAFT
     7→taxon:
     8→  id: NCBITaxon:160488
     9→  label: Pseudomonas putida (strain ATCC 47054 / DSM 6125 / CFBP 8728 / NCIMB 11950
    10→    / KT2440)
    11→description: PP_4983 is an exported FAD-dependent amine oxidase family protein in
    12→  Pseudomonas putida KT2440. It is reviewed here under the alias iaaM because UniProt/GO
    13→  automation assigns tryptophan 2-monooxygenase activity and auxin biosynthesis to
    14→  the locus, but the specific iaaM interpretation is weak. The same locus is annotated
    15→  by KEGG as a putative amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase (K00274, EC 1.4.3.4), and
    16→  KT2440 already contains distinct amino oxidase family members including davB/PP_0383.
    17→  The best current synthesis is therefore monoamine-oxidase-like oxidoreductase activity
    18→  with unresolved physiological substrate, rather than a confirmed indole-3-acetamide
    19→  auxin-biosynthetic enzyme.
    20→existing_annotations:
    21→- term:
    22→    id: GO:0016491
    23→    label: oxidoreductase activity
    24→  evidence_type: IEA
    25→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000120
    26→  review:
    27→    summary: PP_4983 is clearly an oxidoreductase-family protein, but this parent
    28→      term is too broad to be useful. Both UniProt and KEGG place the protein within
    29→      flavin-dependent amine oxidation chemistry, so a more specific amine-oxidase-like
    30→      molecular function is preferable.
    31→    action: MARK_AS_OVER_ANNOTATED
    32→    reason: The broad oxidoreductase root term adds little information once the locus
    33→      is interpreted as a monoamine-oxidase-like flavoprotein. It is directionally
    34→      correct but not an informative core annotation.
    35→    supported_by:
    36→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-notes.md
    37→      supporting_text: The safest current synthesis is amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase-like
    38→        flavoprotein rather than confirmed tryptophan 2-monooxygenase in auxin biosynthesis.
    39→- term:
    40→    id: GO:0050361
    41→    label: tryptophan 2-monooxygenase activity
    42→  evidence_type: IEA
    43→  original_reference_id: GO_REF:0000003
    44→  review:
    45→    summary: This specific iaaM-style annotation is not well supported for PP_4983.
    46→      The UniProt assignment is homology-based only, whereas KEGG classifies the same
    47→      locus as a putative amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase and not as a dedicated
    48→      indole-3-acetamide auxin enzyme. In the same genome, davB/PP_0383 is already
    49→      assigned a distinct amino-oxidase chemistry, reinforcing that automatic family
    50→      transfer is likely overspecific here.
    51→    action: MODIFY
    52→    reason: The protein is probably an amine oxidase, but the specific substrate assignment
    53→      to tryptophan-to-indole-3-acetamide conversion is weaker than the broader monoamine
    54→      oxidase interpretation from KEGG. Until biochemical data establish IAM formation
    55→      in KT2440, the safer molecular function is monoamine oxidase activity.
    56→    proposed_replacement_terms:
    57→    - id: GO:0097621
    58→      label: monoamine oxidase activity
    59→    supported_by:
    60→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-uniprot.txt
    61→      supporting_text: Belongs to the tryptophan 2-monooxygenase family.
    62→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-uniprot.txt
    63→      supporting_text: 'PE   3: Inferred from homology;'
    64→    - reference_id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-notes.md
    65→      supporting_text: KEGG contradicts the specific iaaM call for the same locus
    66→        and instead labels PP_4983 as putative amine oxidase with orthology K00274
    67→        monoamine oxidase [EC:1.4.3.4].
    68→core_functions:
    69→- molecular_function:
    70→    id: GO:0097621
    71→    label: monoamine oxidase activity
    72→  description: 'Best current synthesis: PP_4983 is most likely an exported FAD-dependent
    73→    monoamine oxidase / amine oxidase family protein. The exact in vivo substrate
    74→    remains unresolved, and the available evidence does not justify confident assignment
    75→    to indole-3-acetamide auxin biosynthesis in KT2440.'
    76→  supported_by:
    77→  - reference_id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-notes.md
    78→    supporting_text: The safest current synthesis is amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase-like
    79→      flavoprotein rather than confirmed tryptophan 2-monooxygenase in auxin biosynthesis.
    80→suggested_questions:
    81→- question: Is PP_4983 able to convert L-tryptophan to indole-3-acetamide in vitro
    82→    or in vivo, or is its physiological substrate a different monoamine?
    83→- question: Does KT2440 encode a bona fide iaaH-like amidase that pairs with PP_4983
    84→    in an indole-3-acetamide pathway?
    85→suggested_experiments:
    86→- hypothesis: PP_4983 is a monoamine oxidase-like flavoprotein rather than a dedicated
    87→    iaaM tryptophan 2-monooxygenase.
    88→  description: Purify PP_4983 and test FAD-dependent oxidation of L-tryptophan, methylamine,
    89→    phenethylamine, tyramine, and related amines by LC-MS or targeted product assays.
    90→    Compare turnover against authentic indole-3-acetamide production.
    91→  experiment_type: biochemical enzyme assay
    92→- hypothesis: If PP_4983 participates in IAM/IAA biosynthesis, deleting the locus
    93→    should reduce indole-3-acetamide or indole-3-acetic acid accumulation from tryptophan.
    94→  description: Construct a PP_4983 deletion strain and quantify indole-3-acetamide
    95→    and indole-3-acetic acid in wild type and mutant cultures grown with supplemental
    96→    tryptophan.
    97→  experiment_type: genetics plus metabolomics
    98→- hypothesis: PP_4983 and the other KT2440 amine oxidase candidates have distinct
    99→    substrate scopes.
   100→  description: Compare PP_4983, PP_4155, and davB mutant growth and transcriptional
   101→    responses across candidate amine and amino-acid substrates to separate auxin-pathway
   102→    and general amine-catabolic roles.
   103→  experiment_type: comparative genetics and physiology
   104→references:
   105→- id: GO_REF:0000003
   106→  title: Gene Ontology annotation based on Enzyme Commission mapping
   107→  findings: []
   108→- id: GO_REF:0000120
   109→  title: Combined Automated Annotation using Multiple IEA Methods
   110→  findings: []
   111→- id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-uniprot.txt
   112→  title: UniProt text record for PP_4983 / Q88D45
   113→  findings:
   114→  - statement: UniProt assigns PP_4983 to the tryptophan 2-monooxygenase family by
   115→      homology.
   116→    supporting_text: Belongs to the tryptophan 2-monooxygenase family.
   117→  - statement: The auxin pathway assignment in UniProt is homology-based rather than
   118→      experimental.
   119→    supporting_text: 'PE   3: Inferred from homology;'
   120→- id: file:PSEPK/iaaM/iaaM-notes.md
   121→  title: Notes on the PP_4983 iaaM candidate review
   122→  findings:
   123→  - statement: KEGG assigns PP_4983 as putative amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase
   124→      rather than iaaM.
   125→    supporting_text: KEGG contradicts the specific iaaM call for the same locus and
   126→      instead labels PP_4983 as putative amine oxidase with orthology K00274 monoamine
   127→      oxidase [EC:1.4.3.4].
   128→  - statement: KT2440 contains additional amino oxidase family members, including
   129→      davB/PP_0383.
   130→    supporting_text: KT2440 already contains another amino oxidase family member,
   131→      PP_4155, also labeled putative amine oxidase, and a distinct lysine-pathway
   132→      enzyme davB / PP_0383 assigned K00468 lysine 2-monooxygenase [EC:1.13.12.2].
   133→- id: PMID:12534463
   134→  title: Complete genome sequence and comparative analysis of the metabolically versatile
   135→    Pseudomonas putida KT2440
   136→  full_text_unavailable: true
   137→  findings:
   138→  - statement: Establishes the PP_4983 locus as part of the KT2440 genome sequence.
   139→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# iaaM Candidate Review Notes
     2→
     3→- The review target `iaaM` does not resolve as an exact UniProt gene symbol in PSEPK. The candidate reviewed here is Q88D45 / PP_4983 because its UniProt record carries the automated `tryptophan 2-monooxygenase` and auxin-pathway assignment. [UniProt Q88D45 "DE   RecName: Full=Tryptophan 2-monooxygenase"; "GN   OrderedLocusNames=PP_4983"]
     4→- The PP_4983 assignment to the IAM auxin pathway is entirely automated or homology-based in UniProt: the record says `PE   3: Inferred from homology`, `Belongs to the tryptophan 2-monooxygenase family`, and `PATHWAY: Plant hormone metabolism; auxin biosynthesis.` [UniProt Q88D45 "PE   3: Inferred from homology;"; "Belongs to the tryptophan 2-monooxygenase family."; "PATHWAY: Plant hormone metabolism; auxin biosynthesis."]
     5→- KEGG contradicts the specific iaaM call for the same locus and instead labels PP_4983 as putative amine oxidase with orthology K00274 monoamine oxidase [EC:1.4.3.4]. [KEGG ppu:PP_4983 "NAME        (GenBank) putative amine oxidase"; "ORTHOLOGY   K00274  monoamine oxidase [EC:1.4.3.4]"]
     6→- KT2440 already contains another amino oxidase family member, PP_4155, also labeled putative amine oxidase, and a distinct lysine-pathway enzyme davB / PP_0383 assigned K00468 lysine 2-monooxygenase [EC:1.13.12.2]. [KEGG ppu:PP_4155 "NAME        (GenBank) putative amine oxidase"] [KEGG ppu:PP_0383 "SYMBOL      davB"; "NAME        (GenBank) Lysine 2-monooxygenase"; "ORTHOLOGY   K00468  lysine 2-monooxygenase [EC:1.13.12.2]"]
     7→- The safest current synthesis is amine oxidase / monoamine oxidase-like flavoprotein rather than confirmed tryptophan 2-monooxygenase in auxin biosynthesis.
     8→

Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it looks malicious. If it does, you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer high-level questions about the code behavior.
